steps.title: Identify the two clauses being joined
steps.content:
The sentence contains two independent clauses: (1) "In 2004, Watt sewed strips of blankets together to craft a 10-by-13-inch [blank]" and (2) "in 2014, she arranged folded blankets into two large stacks...". Because no coordinating conjunction connects them, the punctuation in the blank must be strong enough to join two independent clauses on its own — only a semicolon can do this.
  • Choice A — 'sampler later,': comma alone after later creates a comma splice; the adverb later also contradicts the specific date "in 2014" ✗
  • Choice B — 'sampler;': semicolon correctly joins two independent clauses without a coordinating conjunction ✓
  • Choice C — 'sampler,': comma alone creates a comma splice ✗
  • Choice D — 'sampler, later,': comma before later creates a comma splice, and later again contradicts the specific date ✗

steps.title: Confirm the correct choice
steps.content:
A semicolon is the only punctuation mark that can join two independent clauses without a coordinating conjunction. Choices A, C, and D all produce comma splices, and A and D add later which conflicts with the explicit year "in 2014."

That matches choice B — B is correct.
